title: Cardiac Tamponade Recognition and Emergency Management

abridged.title: Abridged Snapshot
abridged.content:
Cardiac tamponade after cardiac surgery

Cardiac tamponade is obstructive shock caused by pressure around the heart limiting diastolic filling. In CTICU it may occur from bleeding, clot, pericardial fluid or localised compression after surgery. Classic Beck’s triad is hypotension, raised JVP/CVP and muffled heart sounds, but post-operative tamponade often does not present classically. A ventilated post-sternotomy patient may show rising CVP, falling MAP, narrowing pulse pressure, tachycardia, low urine output, rising lactate, reduced drain output despite shock, or sudden PEA arrest.

Tamponade — Compression of the heart by pericardial fluid, blood or clot causing impaired ventricular filling and obstructive shock.

Pulsus paradoxus — An exaggerated inspiratory fall in systolic blood pressure, classically >10 mmHg, associated with tamponade but less reliable in ventilated post-op patients.

Finding Why it matters Action
Rising CVP with falling MAP Obstructed filling/RV compression pattern. Urgent senior review; prepare echo and possible return to theatre/resternotomy.
Narrowing pulse pressure Reduced stroke volume. Assess drains, waveform, lactate, UO, echo need.
Sudden reduction in chest drain output plus shock Drain blockage/clot with accumulating blood. Check tubing for kinks/clots; escalate urgently.
PEA after cardiac surgery Tamponade, bleeding, tension pneumothorax, graft failure until proven otherwise. Arrest response with CT surgery-specific pathway.

Suspected tamponade with hypotension, rising lactate, oliguria, escalating vasopressors, falling pulse pressure or PEA is a surgical emergency. Call senior ICU/cardiac surgical help immediately.

expanded.title: Expanded Deep-Dive
expanded.content:
Mechanism

The pericardial space normally contains a small amount of fluid. After cardiac surgery, bleeding or clot can accumulate around the heart. As pressure rises, the lowest-pressure chambers, especially the right atrium and right ventricle, are compressed first. Ventricular filling falls, stroke volume falls, and compensatory tachycardia and vasoconstriction attempt to maintain MAP. Eventually compensation fails and obstructive shock or PEA occurs.

Post-operative tamponade differs from medical tamponade because blood may clot and compress one chamber regionally rather than forming a uniform fluid collection. A loculated clot behind the left atrium or around the right ventricle may cause severe haemodynamic compromise with less obvious global pericardial effusion on transthoracic echo. Transoesophageal echo may be required in ventilated post-op patients.

Beck’s triad is useful but insensitive after surgery. Muffled heart sounds are hard to assess in ICU. JVP is replaced by CVP monitoring but CVP may be confounded by ventilation, RV failure, PEEP, pulmonary hypertension and tricuspid regurgitation. Therefore trend and pattern matter: rising filling pressures with falling output is more important than a single CVP number.

Bedside pattern recognition

pearls.title: Clinical Pearls
pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls

The most dangerous chest drain is not always the one pouring blood out. It may be the one that suddenly stops while the patient becomes shocked.

A normal-looking transthoracic echo does not always exclude post-op localised clot tamponade. If suspicion remains high, keep escalating.

Tamponade can mimic RV failure because both can produce high CVP and low output. The time course, drain behaviour and echo/surgical context help separate them.

Do not try to correct tamponade with escalating noradrenaline alone. Vasopressors may temporarily support MAP but do not restore filling.

PEA in a post-cardiac surgery patient is tamponade or major mechanical/surgical catastrophe until proven otherwise.

equipment.title: Equipment Tie-In
equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In

Chest drains

Chest drain patency is central. Check the entire drainage path from patient to collection system. Look for kinks under bedding, dependent loops, clots in tubing, disconnected suction, incorrect drain height, blocked filters and sudden changes in drainage. Do not strip or aggressively milk drains unless local policy and senior direction explicitly permit it because excessive negative pressure can damage tissue.

Haemodynamic monitor

Monitor sign Tamponade interpretation Action
CVP rising from 8 to 18 mmHg while MAP falls Filling pressure rising but forward flow falling. Escalate urgently.
Arterial waveform pulse pressure narrowing Stroke volume falling. Treat as low output/obstruction pattern.
Tachycardia with falling EtCO2 in arrest Low/no cardiac output. CPR and CT surgery arrest pathway.
PA catheter: equalisation of diastolic pressures Can support tamponade diagnosis. Senior interpretation; do not delay surgical action.

Echo

Echo may show chamber collapse, reduced filling, exaggerated respiratory variation or clot. In post-op patients, windows may be poor and clot may be regional. Use echo as a rapid confirmation tool, not as a reason to ignore a convincing clinical picture.

targets.title: Targets and Numbers
targets.content:
Targets and Numbers

Parameter Concerning threshold / pattern Meaning
Pulse pressure Narrowing, often <25 mmHg or rapidly falling from baseline. Falling stroke volume.
CVP Rising trend, often >15 mmHg in context, but trend more important than number. Impaired filling/RV compression/RV failure pattern.
Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr or abrupt fall. Poor renal perfusion from low output.
Lactate >2 mmol/L and rising; ≥4 mmol/L high concern. Oxygen debt/shock.
Chest drain output Sudden cessation after bleeding plus shock; or excessive bleeding >150–200 mL/hr depending timing/local trigger. Blocked drainage/tamponade or active surgical bleeding.
MAP Usually 65–80 mmHg after routine CABG/valve surgery; often 70–90 mmHg after complex aortic surgery or chronic hypertension if ordered. Treat the number in context: lactate, urine output, mentation/sedation, skin perfusion, SvO2/ScvO2, echo and surgical goals.
Urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; commonly ≥30 mL/hr in average adult; rising concern if <0.3 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours. Low UO with hypotension and narrow pulse pressure suggests low flow until proven otherwise.
Lactate Usually expected to trend down; concern if >2 mmol/L and rising; high concern if ≥4 mmol/L or rising despite support. After CPB, lactate may be elevated, but persistent rise suggests oxygen debt, shock, bleeding, tamponade, severe vasoplegia or poor cardiac output.
Potassium Common rhythm target 4.0–4.5 mmol/L in CTICU unless renal failure/hyperkalaemia risk. Low K increases ectopy, AF, VT risk and digoxin sensitivity.
Magnesium Common rhythm target ≥0.8 mmol/L; many CTICUs target 1.0 mmol/L in AF/VT risk. Low Mg promotes ventricular ectopy and AF; replacement is common post bypass.

quiz.question: Which pattern is most suspicious for post-op tamponade?
options: Rising CVP, falling MAP and narrowing pulse pressure
Stable MAP with improving urine output
quiz.explanation: Tamponade causes obstructed filling: high filling pressure but low forward flow.

quiz.question: Why can chest drain output falling to zero be dangerous after earlier bleeding?
options: The drain may be blocked and blood may be accumulating around the heart
It always means bleeding has stopped safely
It means the patient is ready for discharge
It proves there is no clot
quiz.explanation: Sudden cessation of drainage with shock can indicate blocked drain and tamponade.

quiz.question: Why may post-operative tamponade be missed on transthoracic echo?
options: Clot can be loculated and windows may be poor after surgery
quiz.explanation: Post-surgical clot may compress chambers regionally and TTE windows can be limited.
